  • The Proof
In Loving Memory

A few kind words.

Before we go too far, let us be honest with ourselves: it was a good run. We owe it that much.

May 27, 2003 — April 29, 2026

For WordPress, with love.

It is rare that a piece of software earns the right to be eulogized. Most code dies the way it lived: quietly, in a tarball, on a server nobody is paying for anymore. WordPress will not have that ending. It earned a proper farewell.

It taught a generation of strangers that they could publish on the internet without asking permission. It convinced your aunt that she had a blog. It paid the mortgages of countless freelancers who could install Advanced Custom Fields faster than anyone alive. It powered, at one point, a humbling fraction of the public web — built on PHP, MySQL, and the faith of volunteers.

It survived the death of Flash, the rise of jQuery, the fall of jQuery, the great pivot to React, and at least four different opinions about what a "block" was supposed to be. It outlived Movable Type, Drupal, Joomla, Squarespace's first three logos, and most of the people who said it would be dead by 2010.

And yet. The kindest thing we can say about a tool is that it is no longer needed. WordPress was a workaround for an era when publishing was hard. Publishing is no longer hard. The workaround has done its job.

Go gently into that good /wp-content directory. You did the work. You can rest now.

— Generated, with feeling, in one prompt.

Loved by millions. Replaced by a sentence.
